casestudy.title: KPMG Scenario: National Revenue Authority — D365 Customer Service Contact Centre
casestudy.scenario:
A KPMG client — a National Revenue Authority (NRA) — processes 3,000+ daily taxpayer queries across phone, email, and a web form. Current state: queries handled via email only with no SLA tracking, agents are assigned manually by supervisors, and there is no self-service capability. The NRA has strict data sovereignty requirements under national data protection law — all data must remain in-country.

Your task as the D365 CE Consultant is to architect and lead delivery of a D365 Customer Service contact centre solution.

Requirements:
1. Automated case routing based on query type and agent skill
2. SLA enforcement aligned to a published citizen service charter
3. Real-time visibility dashboards for supervisors
4. Integration with the NRA's legacy taxpayer database (on-premise, REST API, Bearer Token)
5. Data sovereignty compliance — hosting must be within the country's Microsoft data centre
6. Self-service portal for status checking by taxpayers

questions.q: For requirement 1 — automated case routing based on query type and agent skill — what is your recommended architecture?
options: Configure Unified Routing with skill-based routing rules and capacity profiles
Build a PreOperation C# plugin on Case Create that reads query type, queries a custom routing matrix table, and writes the assigned agent before the record is saved
Use Power Automate to update the case owner after save and tolerate temporary misassignment
Use queue routing only and rely on supervisors to rebalance exceptional cases
questions.explanation: For standard skill-based routing requirements, Unified Routing is the first-choice architecture — it is Microsoft's native solution for skills-based, capacity-aware, intelligent work distribution. A custom plugin is the fallback when Unified Routing cannot meet the specific routing matrix complexity (as was the case at NTSA). Always evaluate the native solution first.
wrongExplanations: This is plausible, but the scenario is looking for the custom-routing architecture used to satisfy the stated requirement pattern.
Updating ownership after save introduces an avoidable delay and temporary incorrect assignment state.
This may help operationally, but it does not implement the required routing logic.

questions.q: For requirement 4 — integration with the on-premise legacy taxpayer database via REST API with Bearer Token — what is the correct integration architecture?
options: Call the on-premise API directly from a JavaScript web resource using fetch()
Use an Azure Function as a VPN-connected proxy between D365 cloud and the on-premise system — the Function bridges the cloud-to-private-network boundary and handles Bearer Token authentication
Use an on-premises data gateway or middleware pattern only if it is part of the approved enterprise integration design
Use KingswaySoft to sync the legacy database to Dataverse nightly
questions.explanation: An on-premise system is not directly accessible from D365 cloud. The Azure Function proxy pattern bridges this gap — the Function connects to the private network via VPN Gateway, receives calls from D365 (via Xrm.WebApi or Custom API), authenticates with Bearer Token, calls the legacy system, and returns the result. This is exactly the architecture used at NTSA for iTims.
wrongExplanations: A browser-side web resource is the wrong trust and network boundary for this requirement.
This is a plausible alternative pattern in some estates, but it is not the architecture described by the scenario.
Nightly sync does not satisfy the stated API integration requirement.

questions.q: For requirement 5 — data sovereignty compliance with in-country Microsoft data centre hosting — what must you do before beginning implementation?
options: Select the nearest Azure region during environment creation

Negotiate with Microsoft to confirm the target region hosts data within the country's jurisdiction, verify this satisfies the national data protection law requirements, obtain legal sign-off, and establish the data residency hosting framework before any citizen data enters the platform
Validate the specific Power Platform and Dynamics 365 region behavior, then align legal, security, and client governance approval before production data is introduced
Deploy on-premise D365 to ensure full data control
questions.explanation: Data sovereignty requires proactive negotiation and legal confirmation — not just environment region selection. You must confirm Microsoft's data centre location satisfies the national data protection law, obtain legal and procurement sign-off, and establish the hosting framework before implementation. This was a critical pre-implementation step at NTSA.
wrongExplanations: Nearest is not the same as legally compliant residency.
This is directionally correct, but the expected answer is the fuller governance-and-provider confirmation path expressed in option 2.
That is not the normal response for this cloud-platform scenario.

questions.q: For requirement 6 — a self-service portal for taxpayers to check query status — what is the correct Power Platform technology and what security model must be applied?
options: Model-Driven App shared publicly with anonymous access
Power Pages portal with Web Roles and Table Permissions granting authenticated taxpayers read-only access to their own Case records — preventing access to other taxpayers' data
Canvas App embedded in a website with Dataverse access controlled through the app identity
Power BI dashboard with public sharing enabled
questions.explanation: Power Pages is the correct technology for external-facing portals. The security model must use Web Roles and Table Permissions to ensure each authenticated taxpayer can only see their own Case records — scoped to their contact record. Row-level security through table permissions prevents cross-taxpayer data access, which is critical for a national revenue authority.
wrongExplanations: A model-driven app is not the public self-service pattern being asked for here.
This can present data, but it is not the right self-service portal architecture for this scenario.
Public BI sharing is not a secure taxpayer self-service model.

questions.q: The NRA project has three external vendors: a telephony provider (voice channel), a biometric authentication system, and an SMS gateway. How should you manage the dependency risk across these vendors?
options: Address each vendor dependency as it arises during implementation

Document all vendor dependencies as formal project risks from day one with owners and mitigation plans — ensure all vendors are represented in the initial planning sessions and integration touchpoints are defined upfront
Keep vendor risk tracking informal but review it weekly with the solution architect
Delay integration work until core D365 configuration is complete
questions.explanation: Cross-vendor dependencies must be formally documented as risks from day one with defined owners, mitigation plans, and integration specifications. All vendors should be at the table during initial planning to surface dependencies early. This is the key lesson from NTSA — the AVAYA and QMS dependencies were not documented early enough and became mid-delivery blockers.
wrongExplanations: Reactive handling is too weak for cross-vendor dependencies of this kind.
Informal tracking is usually not strong enough for material multi-vendor delivery risk.
That delays the highest-risk work rather than controlling it.
